retrieved_docs.title: Ginkgo Biloba -- Indications -- Cardiovascular disease (CVD)/ Cardiovascular Risk Factors Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: A randomized controlled trial in 2010 that monitor CVD as a preplanned secondary outcome of the GEM study showed no evidence that ginkgo reduced CV mortality or CVD events though it reported a smaller number of peripheral vascular disease events in ginkgo arm. [15] A systemic review, however, suggested that that Ginkgo biloba had no statistical or clinically significant benefit for patients with peripheral arterial disease. [16] Data from the GEM study also demonstrated that EGb did not reduce blood pressure or the incidence of hypertension in elderly patients with a mean age of 79 years old. [17] Ginkgo biloba research has also shown it to decrease plasma lipoprotein(a) level - a known risk factor for atherosclerotic diseases. [18] A small randomized controlled trial in 2018 showed that adjunct use of EGb along with metformin was more effective than metformin alone in improving outcomes in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus as measured by blood HbA1c, fasting glucose, insulin level, BMI, waist circumference and visceral adiposity index without negatively affecting the liver, kidney, or hematopoietic functions. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Treatment / Management -- Conventional Drugs that Combat Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is commonly used in the treatment of type II diabetic patients with dyslipidemia and low-grade inflammation. The anti-inflammatory activity of metformin is evident by reductions in circulating TNF-alpha, IL-1beta, CRP, and fibrinogen in these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pre-Diabetes and diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Changes in eating patterns with emphasis on a plant-based diet and reduction of animal products and processed foods have also shown benefits in preventing diabetes. ADA recommends metformin to be considered in pre-diabetics with age less than 60 years, history of gestational diabetes mellitus, or BMI ≥ 35 kg/m^2.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Disease Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Secondary Prevention [16]
retrieved_docs.content: A large part of secondary prevention also includes pharmacological therapy. In contrast to primary prevention, anti-thrombotic therapy (low dose aspirin) is strongly recommended unless contraindicated. The daily strength of 75 mg of clopidogrel is recommended for people who are intolerant or allergic to aspirin. Blood pressure should be lowered in all patients with coronary artery disease and stage 1 hypertension using both non- pharmacological and pharmacological therapies. Metformin remains the first-line therapy in diabetic patients for secondary prevention. High intensity or maximally tolerated statin is part of secondary prevention, independent of the lipid levels in as long as the patient can tolerate, and the goal is to achieve LDL less than 70. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Prevention -- Issues of Concern -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes is a major risk factor for ischemic stroke, especially lacunar stroke and large artery stroke. The risk of stroke is tripled in patients with diabetes mellitus of duration over 10 years. Diabetes is a strong risk factor for ischemic stroke and is further associated with other stroke risk factors such as hypertension, abnormal lipid profile, and obesity. So far, there is no proven direct risk between diabetes and primary hemorrhagic stroke. Nevertheless, aggressive diabetic control is prudent. Lifestyle modifications and pharmacological treatment should be initiated for a target glycated hemoglobin of less than 6.5% (48 mmol), along with optimal lipid and blood pressure targets.  Aspirin is recommended for diabetic patients with higher ASCVD risk for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease (CVD), although its role in primary stroke prevention is unclear. Metformin - the first-line medication for type 2 diabetes mellitus patients also has potential benefits for clinical atherosclerotic disease. Some other hypoglycemic agents like SGLT2 inhibitors- empagliflozin, canagliflozin, GLP-1 receptor agonist- liraglutide, thiazolidinedione- pioglitazone, have shown efficacy for reduction of carotid atherosclerosis, and when appropriate diabetic patients with atherosclerotic stroke should receive therapy with such agents. Existing guidelines for glycemic control and lipid and BP targets should be used in diabetic patients who have had a stroke or TIA. [5] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium bicarbonate infusion is recommended for pH < 7.20, especially in the setting of underlying cardiovascular disease or hemodynamic compromise

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The off-label indications of metformin include managing gestational diabetes, addressing weight gain issues caused by antipsychotic medication, preventing type 2 diabetes, and treating and preventing polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Metformin is currently the only ADA-recommended antidiabetic medication used for prediabetes. [2] Furthermore, researchers are studying metformin for its potential antiaging, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Disease Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Type 2 Diabetes mellitus (DM)
retrieved_docs.content: Initially, dietary modifications using a heart-healthy diet (like Mediterranean and DASH diet as mentioned above) and physical activities (at least 150 minutes/week of moderate to vigorous) are encouraged. Additionally, weight loss is recommended if the individual is overweight or obese. Metformin can also be considered as first-line therapy for type 2 DM to improve the glycemic index and reduce cardiovascular risk. If the HbA1c remains over 7% with lifestyle modification and metformin, then the addition of SGLT–2 inhibitors or GLP-1 agonists can be considered as they have shown to reduce the ASCVD risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pre-Diabetes and diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin should be temporarily discontinued before iodinated contrast exposure in patients with eGFR <60. The addition of other medications to metformin is dependent on co-morbidities and glycemic control after maximally tolerated metformin therapy. Generally speaking, the glycemic target is to achieve HbA1c < 7% in most patients with diabetes. However, there is increasing evidence that glycemic targets should be individualized. Glycemic targets can be higher (HbA1c <8%) in older people, people with limited life expectancy, and those with a significant risk of hypoglycemia. Conversely, lower glycemic targets might be appropriate and even beneficial in younger patients and those without risk of significant hypoglycemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Appropriate Use of SGLT2s and GLP-1 RAs With Insulin to Reduce CVD Risk in Patients With Diabetes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- The American Diabetes Association Guideline for Pharmacologic Therapy for Type 2 Diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is the preferred initial pharmacologic agent for therapy and should be continued unless it is not tolerated or there is a contraindication for its use. Other therapies should be added to metformin when additional therapy is required. Insulin should be started in addition to metformin is if there is ongoing catabolism (weight loss) or if hemoglobin A1C level is greater than 10%. For patients who have established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease or are at high risk for this disease or heart failure, an SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1RA should be initiated early regardless of the hemoglobin A1C level. In patients with type 2 diabetes, GLP-1RA is preferred to insulin when possible. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Alogliptin -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The ADA's Standards of Care in Diabetes currently considers DPP-4 inhibitors an add-on therapy for patients who do not achieve their A1C goal after 3 months of lifestyle modifications and metformin. DPP-4 inhibitors are valuable in both dual and triple therapy. Current guidelines emphasize selecting a second agent based on the patient's RISK OF atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). If a patient has ASCVD, clinicians should select an agent with proven cardiovascular benefits and mortality reduction. Alogliptin has not demonstrated a reduction in cardiovascular risks or mortality, and concerns exist regarding a potential increased risk of heart failure with DPP-4 inhibitors. Given that nearly half of all patients with T2D develop heart failure during their lifetime, this remains a critical consideration in treatment selection.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiologic and Etiologic Considerations of Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- Medical Factors of Obesity
retrieved_docs.content: The use of intensive insulin therapy and home glucose monitoring often manages diabetes. Tight control of blood glucose is critical in preventing the vascular complications of the condition. However, insulin therapy itself is associated with weight gain. [54] [55] Some studies have suggested that metformin may result in weight loss in patients with type 2 diabetes. However, the evidence to establish this association is limited. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Appropriate Use of SGLT2s and GLP-1 RAs With Insulin to Reduce CVD Risk in Patients With Diabetes (Archived) -- Clinical Significance -- Dyslipidemia
retrieved_docs.content: In terms of diabetic medications and their effects on lipids, metformin is associated with lowering triglyceride levels and an increase in high-density lipoprotein (HDL) levels. It appears that metformin may work through various pleiotropic cellular and molecular mechanisms which work to alter atherogenesis directly. Another glucose-lowering medication used for type 2 diabetes mellitus is the dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) medication, which has also been shown to alter lipid profiles beneficially.

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin Resistance -- Treatment / Management -- Specific Pharmacological Interventions for Blood Glucose Management
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is a common first-line therapy for medication treatment of T2D and is approved for use in PCOS. The DPP & DPPOS study showed that the combination of metformin and lifestyle interventions was medically useful and cost-effective. Despite the concerns about using metformin in mild to moderate renal dysfunction, several organizations, including the American Geriatric Society and the Kidney Disease Improving Global Outcomes guidelines, endorse use as long as the GFR exceeds 30. [24] [26] [43] [44] [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Older patients: For individuals of advanced age, it is recommended to begin with a lower initial and maintenance dose as a precaution. This patient population may exhibit decreased renal function, making it crucial for physicians to adjust the dose based on a thorough assessment of their renal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1898
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is classified as a member of the biguanide blood‑glucose‑lowering drug class, which comprises agents used to reduce blood glucose levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 134
retrieved_docs.content: drugs” both reside under the overarching class of blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins, with metformin as a specific agent within the biguanide subclass. This knowledge base integrates the therapeutic categories of glucose‑lowering and lipid‑lowering agents, highlights fixed‑dose combinations with long‑acting insulins, and underscores important drug‑interaction contraindications relevant to patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: and the insulin secretagogue repaglinide. The “biguanide blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs” subclass comprises fenformin and metformin, with metformin explicitly identified as a biguanide. Lipid‑modifying therapy is represented by the plain fibrate class, which contains bezafibrát, klofibrát and gemfibrozil. Bezafibrát and klofibrát each produce decreased lipolysis, contributing to reduced circulating free fatty acids and improved lipid profiles. Gemfibrozil is linked to the formulation group “G [Preparations]” as its parent preparation and is contraindicated when co‑administered with repaglinide, highlighting a clinically important drug‑interaction risk in patients receiving both lipid‑lowering and glucose‑lowering treatments. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications delineate the specific agents within each diabetes‑related class, their molecular targets and physiologic effects, and key therapeutic interactions that inform safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2650
retrieved_docs.content: The class of biguanide blood glucose‑lowering drugs includes both fenformin and metformin, each of which is a member of this drug category that is used to lower blood glucose levels in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Lipid modifying agents are classified as a subset of cardiovascular system drugs, meaning they belong to the broader group of medications used to treat conditions of the cardiovascular system. As cardiovascular system drugs, lipid modifying agents are employed to manage abnormal lipid levels, lower cholesterol, and reduce the risk of atherosclerotic disease, thereby contributing to overall cardiovascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 873
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha glucosidase inhibitors, blood glucose lowering and biguanide blood glucose lower drugs are both classified as blood glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins, indicating that they belong to the broader category of non‑insulin agents used to reduce blood glucose levels. Within the biguanide blood glucose lower drugs class, the specific agents metformin and fenformin are members, highlighting that these individual drugs are examples of biguanide‑type glucose‑lowering medications. Together, these drug classes and their constituent agents represent important therapeutic options for managing hyperglycemia in diabetes without the use of insulin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 147
retrieved_docs.content: specific cardiovascular diseases, inflammatory pathways, lipid‑modifying and hypoglycemic agents, anticoagulants, and related oncologic and pancreatic conditions intersect, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: drugs used in diabetes. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in selecting appropriate combination therapies, recognizing the mechanisms of action of individual agents, and optimizing patient‑centered diabetes care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: injectable insulins, some combined with GLP‑1 analogues) and a diverse array of non‑insulin glucose‑lowering drugs, encompassing GLP‑1 analogues, DPP‑4 inhibitors, alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, and biguanides such as metformin and phenformin. Understanding these hierarchical relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacologic strategies and in recognizing combination products that integrate multiple mechanisms of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines “DRUGS USED IN DIABETES” as comprising two major super‑classes: **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES** and **BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS**. Within INSULINS AND ANALOGUES, the subclass **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES FOR INJECTION, LONG‑ACTING** includes agents such as **INSULIN GLARGINE**, the combination product **INSULIN DEGLUDEC/LIRAGLUTIDE** (which incorporates **LIRAGLUTIDE**), and **INSULIN GLARGINE/LIXISENATIDE** (which incorporates **LIXISENATIDE**). Non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents are organized into several pharmacologic classes. **ALPHA GLUCOSIDASE INHIBITORS** lower blood glucose by delaying carbohydrate absorption. **DIPEPTIDYL PEPTIDASE‑4 (DPP‑4) INHIBITORS**—including **ALOGLIPTIN** and **SITAGLIPTIN** (the latter also listed as a DPP‑4 inhibitor)—are classified under BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS. **GLUCAGON‑LIKE PEPTIDE‑1 (GLP‑1) ANALOGUES** comprise **EXENATID**, **LIRAGLUTIDE**, and **LIXISENATIDE**. **BIGUANIDE BLOOD GLUCOSE LOWER DRUGS** represent another subclass; **FENFORMIN (metformin)** is a member, illustrating that biguanides are a subset of the broader non‑insulin glucose‑lowering category. Combination formulations link agents from different classes, exemplified by **SIMVASTATIN/SITAGLIPTIN**, which pairs the statin **SIMVASTATIN** with the DPP‑4 inhibitor **SITAGLIPTIN**. An important drug‑interaction contraindication is noted: **NELFINAVIR MESYLATE** is contraindicated when used with **SIMVASTATIN**. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies that insulin preparations (including long‑acting analogues) and various oral or injectable non‑insulin agents (alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, GLP‑1 analogues, and biguanides) are organized under the overarching category of diabetes medications, with combination products and specific contraindications guiding safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: of BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS EXCLUDING INSULINS. This group includes METFORMIN (the prototypical biguanide) and its misspelled variant FENFORMIN, both of which act by decreasing hepatic glucose production and improving insulin sensitivity. Overall, the taxonomy links insulin‑based injectable therapies, GLP‑1 receptor agonists, alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, and biguanides under the broader umbrella of diabetes pharmacotherapy, highlighting their hierarchical relationships and the ways in which combination products integrate agents from different subclasses to achieve comprehensive glycemic control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate is classified as a member of the fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As part of this drug class, bezafibrate shares the typical pharmacologic actions of fibrates, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2216
retrieved_docs.content: Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients who have ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels), indicating that the use of medium chain triglycerides should be avoided in individuals with this condition because it may exacerbate or is not recommended for managing high triglyceride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 112
retrieved_docs.content: organized under “blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins” and include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors (alogliptin, sitagliptin), and GLP‑1 analogues (liraglutide, lixisenatide). A further subclass, biguanides, comprises metformin and fenformin, which share a common chemical scaffold and glucose‑lowering activity. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how glycerol is shared across diverse formulations and how diabetes medications are systematically categorized by mechanism, molecular target, and pharmacologic profile to guide clinical use and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1969
retrieved_docs.content: Bempedoic acid is a pharmacologic agent that is not classified as a hypoglycemic drug, indicating it does not lower blood glucose levels. Instead, bempedoic acid may be employed in the treatment of type II hyperlipidemia, a lipid disorder characterized by elevated cholesterol and triglyceride levels. This distinction highlights bempedoic acid’s role in managing dyslipidemia rather than glycemic control, which is important for clinicians selecting appropriate therapies for patients with abnormal lipid profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 772
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha glucosidase inhibitors, blood glucose lowering are classified as blood glucose lowering drugs, excl. insulins, indicating that they belong to the non‑insulin category of agents used to reduce blood glucose. Biguanide blood glucose lower drugs are likewise categorized as blood glucose lowering drugs, excl. insulins, placing them in the same therapeutic group. Metformin is a member of the biguanide blood glucose lower drugs class, representing a specific drug within this subclass. Together, alpha glucosidase inhibitors and biguanide agents such as metformin provide alternative, non‑insulin mechanisms for managing hyperglycemia in patients with diabetes.
